我猜一切的未来都是谎言：新工作

The Future of Everything Is Lies, I Guess: New Jobs

aphyr.com 作者 aphyr 分数 246 评论 167 2026/04/15 21:34
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 目录 这是一篇很长的文章，因此我将其分成一系列帖子，并将在接下来的几天内发布。您还可以阅读 PDF 或 EPUB 格式的完整作品；这些文件将随着每个部分的发布而更新。简介 动态 文化信息 生态烦恼 心理危害 安全工作 新岗位 我们何去何从
  2. 上一篇：工作。随着我们更广泛地部署机器学习，将会出现新的工作类型。我认为其中大部分将发生在人类和机器学习系统之间的边界。咒语者可以专注于提示模型。流程和统计工程师可以控制机器学习输出周围的系统和模型本身的错误。现在有数量惊人的人被聘为模型培训师，将他们的人类专业知识输入到自动化系统中。当机器学习系统失败时，肉盾可能需要承担责任，而哈斯皮斯可以解释模型的行为。 Incanters LLM 很奇怪。有时，你可以通过威胁他们、告诉他们他们是专家、重复你的命令或对他们撒谎说他们会获得经济奖金来获得更好的结果。它们的性能会随着输入时间的延长而降低，并且对一项任务有帮助的令牌可能会污染另一项任务，因此优秀的 LLM 用户会考虑很多关于限制输入模型的上下文。我想可能会有人（从事各种工作！）专门知道如何为法学硕士提供带来良好结果的投入。软件行业的一些人似乎正朝着这个方向发展：成为与克劳德交谈的法学硕士咒语者，而不是直接使用代码的程序员。工艺工程师 LLM 输出的不可预测性需要质量控制。例如，律师因为在法庭上提交人工智能捏造的内容而不断陷入麻烦。如果他们想继续使用法学硕士，律师事务所将需要某种流程工程师来帮助他们发现法学硕士错误。你可以想象这样一个过程：撰写法庭文件的人故意插入微妙的（但很容易纠正的）错误，并删除应该存在的内容。这些引入的错误将被注册以供以后使用。然后，该文档被传递给编辑，编辑会仔细审阅该文档，但不知道引入了哪些错误。只有发现所有故意错误（希望是意外错误）后，该文件才能离开公司。我想象出处跟踪软件、与 LexisNexis 和文档工作流程系统的集成等等来支持这种质量控制工作流程。这些流程工程师将帮助构建和调整质量控制流程：培训人员、确定需要额外审查的地方、调整自动化支持的水平、衡量整个流程是否比手工工作更好，等等。统计工程师 一个密切相关的角色可能是统计工程师：尝试直接测量、建模和控制机器学习系统中的可变性的人。例如，统计工程师可能会发现，法学硕士在看到一系列选项时所做的选择会受到这些选项出现顺序的影响，并开发出补偿方法。我怀疑这可能看起来有点像心理测量学——在这个领域，心理学家竭尽全力通过间接手段对人类的混乱行为进行统计建模和测量。由于法学硕士是混沌系统，这项工作将是复杂且具有挑战性的：模型不会简单地“95％准确”。相反，用于数据库查询的 ML 优化器可能在英文文本上表现良好，但在时间序列数据上表现不佳。医疗保健法学硕士对于英语查询可能非常准确，但当用西班牙语提出同样的问题时，其表现却很糟糕。这将需要深入的、特定领域的工作。模型训练器随着互联网的普及，实验室可能很难获得用于训练模型的高质量语料库。培训师还必须应对虚假消息来源：Almira Osmanovic Thunström 证明，只要少数明显虚假的文章1，就可能导致 Gemini、ChatGPT 和 Copilot 向用户告知一种具有可笑名称的虚构疾病。有经济、文化和政治动机来影响法学硕士的言论；似乎可以肯定地认为，未来的语料库将越来越多地受到错误信息的污染。一种解决方案是使用低本底钢的信息等效物：2023 年之前生产的未受污染的作品更有可能准确。另一种选择是聘请人类专家作为模型训练员。 OpenAI 可以聘请加洛林文艺复兴时期的博士后来教授他们的模型有关阿尔昆的知识。这些主题专家将为初始训练阶段编写文档，制定评估基准，并在调节过程中检查模型的响应。法学硕士也容易犯看似正确的细微错误。也许解决这个问题需要聘请非常聪明的人来仔细阅读大量法学硕士的成果并找出其中的错误。在另一个“我几年前写的，现在已经是常识”的案例中，一位朋友向我介绍了 Mercor、Scale AI 等公司的这篇文章，这些公司雇佣了大量专业人士来训练模型来完成神秘的任务——可能会在这个过程中让他们自己失业。 “正如一位行业资深人士所说，有史以来最大的人类专业知识收获。”当然，还有老板软件、工资缩减、荒谬的工作时间和没有工会。2 肉盾 你可能会认为首席执行官和董事会成员可能担心自己的工作可能会被法学硕士取代，但这似乎并没有阻止他们使用“人工智能”作为解雇大量员工的借口。我认为部分原因是这些角色不仅仅是发送电子邮件和查看图表，而且还需要在法律体系和舆论的胃中悬挂一个温暖的身体。你可以对使用法学硕士的公司处以罚款，但只有人类才能道歉或入狱。人类可以受到后果的激励，并以法学硕士无法做到的方式提供社会补救。我正在思考《芝加哥太阳时报》夏季插页草率的后果。任何读过这篇文章的人都应该意识到这是无稽之谈，但芝加哥公共媒体首席执行官梅丽莎·贝尔解释说，他们从赫斯特旗下的 King Features 获取这篇文章，想必赫斯特所发表的文章不完全是由锯末和谎言组成的。 King Features 则表示，他们将整个 64 页的插页转包给了自由撰稿人 Marco Buscaglia。当然，巴斯卡利亚与法学硕士关系最密切，并承担着重大责任，但与此同时，培训法学硕士的人也为这一愚蠢行为做出了贡献，King Features 和《太阳时报》的编辑以及间接地，他们各自的经理也是如此。这些人叫什么名字？他们为什么不像布斯卡利亚和贝尔那样道歉？我认为我们会看到一些人被雇用（尽管可能没有明确）作为肉盾：在他们的监督下对机器学习系统负责的人。问责制可能纯粹是内部的，就像 Meta 雇用人员来审查自动审核系统的决策一样。它可能是外部的，例如律师因向法院提交法学硕士谎言而受到惩罚。它可能涉及正式的职责，例如数据保护官。对于公司来说，拥有像 Buscaglia 这样的第三方分包商可能会很方便，当整个系统出现问题时，他们可能会被抛弃。也许大多数自动驾驶汽车发生事故的司机也会以同样的方式承担责任——马德琳·克莱尔·埃利什（Madeline Clare Elish）将这个概念称为道德溃缩区。写完这篇文章后，我突然想到国会听证会采访大型语言模型的景象。 “你说得完全正确，参议员。我确实挪用了那六千五百万美元。故障如下……” Haruspices 当模型出错时，我们想知道原因。是什么导致无人机放弃预定目标并在野战医院引爆？为什么医疗保健模型不太可能准确诊断黑人？当其中一辆车碾压儿童时，自动出租车公司应该承担多大的责任？为什么社交媒体公司的自动审核系统不断将《大金刚》的屏幕截图标记为裸体？这些任务可能会落到 haruspex 身上：负责筛选模型的输入、输出和内部状态，试图综合解释其行为。其中一些工作将是对单个案例的深入调查，而其他情况则需要 ML 公司、其用户、独立记者、法院和 NTSB 等机构在内部部署 Haruspices。
  3. 当我说“显然”时，我的意思是该论文包含“整篇论文都是编造的”阶段。再说一次，LLM 都是白痴。 ↩
  4. 此时，请读者脱口而出“真正的问题是资本主义！”的尖叫声。他们一直在保留前面的二十七页。我就在你身边。尽管如此，核危机和环境破坏从来不只限于资本主义国家。如果您有朋友或亲戚住在（例如）苏联，那么询问他们认为政治局会用这项技术做什么可能会很有趣。 ↩

Original Summary

  1. Table of Contents This is a long article, so I'm breaking it up into a series of posts which will be released over the next few days. You can also read the full work as a PDF or EPUB; these files will be updated as each section is released. Introduction Dynamics Culture Information Ecology Annoyances Psychological Hazards Safety Work New Jobs Where Do We Go From Here
  2. Previously: Work. As we deploy ML more broadly, there will be new kinds of work. I think much of it will take place at the boundary between human and ML systems. Incanters could specialize in prompting models. Process and statistical engineers might control errors in the systems around ML outputs and in the models themselves. A surprising number of people are now employed as model trainers, feeding their human expertise to automated systems. Meat shields may be required to take accountability when ML systems fail, and haruspices could interpret model behavior. Incanters LLMs are weird. You can sometimes get better results by threatening them, telling them they’re experts, repeating your commands, or lying to them that they’ll receive a financial bonus. Their performance degrades over longer inputs, and tokens that were helpful in one task can contaminate another, so good LLM users think a lot about limiting the context that’s fed to the model. I imagine that there will probably be people (in all kinds of work!) who specialize in knowing how to feed LLMs the kind of inputs that lead to good results. Some people in software seem to be headed this way: becoming LLM incanters who speak to Claude, instead of programmers who work directly with code. Process Engineers The unpredictable nature of LLM output requires quality control. For example, lawyers keep getting in trouble because they submit AI confabulations in court. If they want to keep using LLMs, law firms are going to need some kind of process engineers who help them catch LLM errors. You can imagine a process where the people who write a court document deliberately insert subtle (but easily correctable) errors, and delete things which should have been present. These introduced errors are registered for later use. The document is then passed to an editor who reviews it carefully without knowing what errors were introduced. The document can only leave the firm once all the intentional errors (and hopefully accidental ones) are caught. I imagine provenance-tracking software, integration with LexisNexis and document workflow systems, and so on to support this kind of quality-control workflow. These process engineers would help build and tune that quality-control process: training people, identifying where extra review is needed, adjusting the level of automated support, measuring whether the whole process is better than doing the work by hand, and so on. Statistical Engineers A closely related role might be statistical engineers: people who attempt to measure, model, and control variability in ML systems directly. For instance, a statistical engineer could figure out that the choice an LLM makes when presented with a list of options is influenced by the order in which those options were presented, and develop ways to compensate. I suspect this might look something like psychometrics—a field in which psychologists have gone to great lengths to statistically model and measure the messy behavior of humans via indirect means. Since LLMs are chaotic systems, this work will be complex and challenging: models will not simply be “95% accurate”. Instead, an ML optimizer for database queries might perform well on English text, but pathologically on timeseries data. A healthcare LLM might be highly accurate for queries in English, but perform abominably when those same questions are presented in Spanish. This will require deep, domain-specific work. Model Trainers As slop takes over the Internet, labs may struggle to obtain high-quality corpuses for training models. Trainers must also contend with false sources: Almira Osmanovic Thunström demonstrated that just a handful of obviously fake articles1 could cause Gemini, ChatGPT, and Copilot to inform users about an imaginary disease with a ridiculous name. There are financial, cultural, and political incentives to influence what LLMs say; it seems safe to assume future corpuses will be increasingly tainted by misinformation. One solution is to use the informational equivalent of low-background steel: uncontaminated works produced prior to 2023 are more likely to be accurate. Another option is to employ human experts as model trainers. OpenAI could hire, say, postdocs in the Carolingian Renaissance to teach their models all about Alcuin. These subject-matter experts would write documents for the initial training pass, develop benchmarks for evaluation, and check the model’s responses during conditioning. LLMs are also prone to making subtle errors that look correct. Perhaps fixing that problem involves hiring very smart people to carefully read lots of LLM output and catch where it made mistakes. In another case of “I wrote this years ago, and now it’s common knowledge”, a friend introduced me to this piece on Mercor, Scale AI, et al., which employ vast numbers of professionals to train models to do mysterious tasks—presumably putting themselves out of work in the process. “It is, as one industry veteran put it, the largest harvesting of human expertise ever attempted.” Of course there’s bossware, and shrinking pay, and absurd hours, and no union.2 Meat Shields You would think that CEOs and board members might be afraid that their own jobs could be taken over by LLMs, but this doesn’t seem to have stopped them from using “AI” as an excuse to fire lots of people. I think a part of the reason is that these roles are not just about sending emails and looking at graphs, but also about dangling a warm body over the maws of the legal system and public opinion. You can fine an LLM-using corporation, but only humans can apologize or go to jail. Humans can be motivated by consequences and provide social redress in a way that LLMs can’t. I am thinking of the aftermath of the Chicago Sun-Times’ sloppy summer insert. Anyone who read it should have realized it was nonsense, but Chicago Public Media CEO Melissa Bell explained that they sourced the article from King Features, which is owned by Hearst, who presumably should have delivered articles which were not composed entirely of sawdust and lies. King Features, in turn, says they subcontracted the entire 64-page insert to freelancer Marco Buscaglia. Of course Buscaglia was most proximate to the LLM and bears significant responsibility, but at the same time, the people who trained the LLM contributed to this tomfoolery, as did the editors at King Features and the Sun-Times, and indirectly, their respective managers. What were the names of those people, and why didn’t they apologize as Buscaglia and Bell did? I think we will see some people employed (though perhaps not explicitly) as meat shields: people who are accountable for ML systems under their supervision. The accountability may be purely internal, as when Meta hires human beings to review the decisions of automated moderation systems. It may be external, as when lawyers are penalized for submitting LLM lies to the court. It may involve formalized responsibility, like a Data Protection Officer. It may be convenient for a company to have third-party subcontractors, like Buscaglia, who can be thrown under the bus when the system as a whole misbehaves. Perhaps drivers whose mostly-automated cars crash will be held responsible in the same way—Madeline Clare Elish calls this concept a moral crumple zone. Having written this, I am suddenly seized with a vision of a congressional hearing interviewing a Large Language Model. “You’re absolutely right, Senator. I did embezzle those sixty-five million dollars. Here’s the breakdown…” Haruspices When models go wrong, we will want to know why. What led the drone to abandon its intended target and detonate in a field hospital? Why is the healthcare model less likely to accurately diagnose Black people? How culpable should the automated taxi company be when one of its vehicles runs over a child? Why does the social media company’s automated moderation system keep flagging screenshots of Donkey Kong as nudity? These tasks could fall to a haruspex: a person responsible for sifting through a model’s inputs, outputs, and internal states, trying to synthesize an account for its behavior. Some of this work will be deep investigations into a single case, and other situations will demand broader statistical analysis. Haruspices might be deployed internally by ML companies, by their users, independent journalists, courts, and agencies like the NTSB.
  3. When I say “obviously”, I mean the paper included the phase “this entire paper is made up”. Again, LLMs are idiots. ↩
  4. At this point the reader is invited to blurt out whatever screams of “the real problem is capitalism!” they have been holding back for the preceding twenty-seven pages. I am right there with you. That said, nuclear crisis and environmental devastation were never limited to capitalist nations alone. If you have a friend or relative who lived in (e.g.) the USSR, it might be interesting to ask what they think the Politburo would have done with this technology. ↩

评论（默认中文）

ej88 2026/04/15 22:33
我个人认为，机器学习最终将成为“正常技术”，尽管具有令人难以置信的变革性。

我认为你可以将“Incanters”和“流程工程师”合并为一个“用户”。包含需要问责制角色的工作将是指导、提供背景并验证代理的输出，几乎就像数百万工人了解基本的计算机技能和 Microsoft Office 一样。

在我看来，LLM时代的工作风险有多大，可以归结为：

1：构建 RL 循环以提高性能爬山有多容易？

2：构建 LLM 工具来执行任务有多容易？

3：工作中有多少是结构化任务与承担责任？错误的后果是什么？其中有多少可以归结为人际关系？

这就是为什么我一直非常看好软件工程（但不看好编码）。您可以轻松地在人为或沙盒编码任务上设置 1) 和 2)，但随后 3) 会扩展并主导角色的其余部分。

关于模型训练师——我不太相信 RLHF 会让专业专家失业，原因有几个。首先，几乎所有人类数据公司产生的数据都有些人为，根据定义，让人们在承包平台上对输出进行评分；另外，我们在世界上收集的数据量似乎是无限的。其次，正如我之前提到的，瓶颈既在于问责制，又在于模型无错误地找到新上下文的能力。

I am personally of the opinion that ML will end up being 'normal technology', albeit incredibly transformative.

I think you can combine 'Incanters' and 'Process Engineers' into one - 'Users'. Jobs that encompass a role that requires accountability will be directing, providing context, and verifying the output of agents, almost like how millions of workers know basic computer skills and Microsoft Office.

In my opinion, how at-risk a job is in the LLM era comes down to:

1: How easy is it to construct RL loops to hillclimb on performance?

2: How easy is it to construct a LLM harness to perform the tasks?

3: How much of the job is a structured set of tasks vs. taking accountability? What's the consequence of a mistake? How much of it comes down to human relationships?

Hence why I've been quite bullish on software engineering (but not coding). You can easy set up 1) and 2) on contrived or sandboxed coding tasks but then 3) expands and dominates the rest of the role.

On Model Trainers -- I'm not so convinced that RLHF puts the professional experts out of work, for a few reasons. Firstly, nearly all human data companies produce data that is somewhat contrived, by definition of having people grade outputs on a contracting platform; plus there's a seemingly unlimited bound on how much data we can harvest in the world. Secondly, as I mentioned before, the bottleneck is both accountability and the ability for the model to find fresh context without error.

在 HN 中查看
aphyr 2026/04/16 00:09
> 我认为你可以将“Incanters”和“Process Engineers”合二为一——“Users”

我想更多地谈论这一点，但不太清楚如何表达它，所以我删掉了一点：用“咒语”，我试图指出一种……直观的、更非正式的从业者知识/metis，并将其与“统计/过程工程师”中更严格的统计方法进行对比。我预计很多人会将两者融合在一起，但我正试图在这里建立一些支柱。用户整合一系列方法，包括个人直觉、民间传说、正式和非正式文本、科学论文以及严格设计的工具和内部实验。就像农业一样，对当地气候和地方品种有深入、直观的了解，也有大型工业实践和研究地块，这些不同的方法以复杂的方式相互告知（和覆盖）。

> I think you can combine 'Incanters' and 'Process Engineers' into one - 'Users'

I wanted to talk about this more but couldn't quite figure out how to phrase it, so I cut a fair bit: with "incanters" I'm trying to point at a sort of ... intuitive, more informal practitioner knowledge / metis, and contrast it with a more statistically rigorous approach in "statistical/process engineers". I expect a lot of people will fuse the two, but I'm trying to stake out some tentpoles here. Users integrate a continuum of approaches, including individual intuition, folklore, formal and informal texts, scientific papers, and rigorously designed harnesses & in-house experiments. Like farming--there's deep, intuitive knowledge of local climate and landraces, but also big industrial practice, and also research plots, and those different approaches inform (and override) each other in complex ways.

在 HN 中查看
netcan 2026/04/15 22:53
从某种意义上说，技术无论如何都是“不正常的”。

如果我们想到数字化技术革命……即使是现在，它对经济带来的变化也很难描述清楚。

早期，它将把银行从十亿美元的业务转变为百万美元的业务。大学将能够消除大部分管理人员。会计和财务将变得微不足道。 ETC。

早期的技术革命也是不可预测的……但至少回顾起来它们是有意义的。

它不是 那 清楚我们经济的核心活动是什么。在微观层面上它是清晰的，但当你缩小时它就会变得模糊。

为什么需要问责制？它在其上下文中显然是需要的......但很难理解它是如何聚合的。

In some sense, technology is "not normal" regardless.

If we think of the digitization tech revolution... the changes it made to the economy are hard to describe well, even now.

In the early days, it was going to turn banks from billion dollar businesses to million dollar ones. Universities would be able to eliminate most of their admin. Accounting and finances would be trivialized. Etc.

Earlier tech revolution s were unpredictable too... But at lest retrospectively they made sense.

It's not that clear what the core activities of our economy even are. It's clear at micro level, but as you zoom out it gets blurry.

Why is accountability needed? It's clearly needed in its context... but it's hard to understand how it aggregates.

在 HN 中查看
bobthepanda 2026/04/15 22:56
问责制实际上是解决责任的一种方式。只要人们可以起诉，公司可以赔偿，或者个人可以入狱，责任问题就永远存在；从历史上看，法院并没有善意地对待那些举起双手并说“我只是遵循人类/实体的命令”的人
Accountability is really a way to address liability. So long as people can sue and companies can pay out, or individuals can go to jail, there is always going to be a question of liability; and historically the courts have not looked kindly at those who throw their hands up in the air and say “I was just following orders from a human/entity”
在 HN 中查看
netcan 2026/04/16 06:37
是的。我同意。有一个上下文，并且在这个上下文中它是有意义的。但是，经济或工业并不能产生问责制、责任和法庭可读性。这些是支持结构，而不是最终目标。

我这样说是为了回应上面的例子，当责任感受到关注时，就需要人类。这与宏观相差甚远。

如果我们想想 19 世纪的经济……它主要涉及食品、家居产品等。现在，经济变得更加难以推理，而且在谈论技术将如何影响经济时，很容易只见树木、不见森林。

与您的支付处理器合作需要问责制，该支付处理器与 Visa 和 Mastercard 一起使用，也有要求等。根据范式转变发生的地点（任何地方）......我们可能需要也可能不需要这些功能。

这就是为什么我们很难通过推理来预测即将到来的人工智能介导的变化。

Yes. I agree. There is a context and it makes sense within this context. But, the economy or industry is not there to produce accountability, liability, court-legibility. These are support structures, not end goals.

I said this in response to the example above, that humans are needed where accountability is a concern. This is pretty distant from the macro.

If we think of the 19th century economy... it was mostly about food, household products and suchlike. Now the economy is a lot harder to reason about and it's easy to miss the forrest for the trees... when talking about how technology will affect it.

Accountability is required to work with your payment processor, which works with visa and mastercard, that also have requirements, etc. Depending on where (of anywhere) paradigm shifts occur... we may or may not even need these functions.

That's why it's so hard to reason our way to predictions about upcoming Ai-mediated changes.

在 HN 中查看
pixl97 2026/04/15 23:19
>从历史上看，法院没有审查过

这取决于法院系统是否不受腐败影响。我们已经看到“太大而不能倒”类别的大公司不受政府控制。在贿赂/游说合法化或被忽视的国家，他们有资金占领法庭。

>nd historically the courts have not looked

This is dependent on having a court system uncaptured by corruption. We're already seeing that large corporations in the "too big to fail" categories fall outside of government control. And in countries with bribing/lobbying legalized or ignored they have the funds to capture the courts.

在 HN 中查看
bobthepanda 2026/04/15 23:48
虽然这是事实，但由于很少有行业真正处于垄断状态，而且大公司也会互相起诉，这一事实在一定程度上缓解了这种情况。
While this is true, this is somewhat mitigated by the fact that few sectors are truly monopolized and large corporations also sue each other.
在 HN 中查看
mlsu 2026/04/15 23:57
比如说，一个人如何承担侵犯版权的责任？
Sort if like how one could be held liable for copyright infringement?
在 HN 中查看
cucumber3732842 2026/04/15 23:28
强制性（通过法规或事实上的相邻法规，例如强制性保险及其要求）专业执照的一个重要组成部分是，如果您遵守政府（代表的某些实体）制定的规则，作为回报，政府将永远不会让您承担责任。政府获得部分控制，其控制下的人民得到部分保护。

“哦，我很抱歉你们医院烧死了普兰蒂夫先生，但电工遵循他的专业规则，所以他的责任上限为<小数字>，你只需要吃掉这个”

我敢打赌，在这样的某种安排下，至少有一半的经济存在。

A huge component of compulsory (either by statute or de-facto as a result of adjacent statute, like mandatory insurance + requirements thereof) professional licensure is that if you follow the rules set by (some entity deputized by) government the government will in return never leave you holding the bag. The government gains partial control and the people under it's control get partial protection.

"oh I'm sorry your hospital burned down mr plantiff but the electrician was following his professional rules so his liability is capped at <small number> you'll just have to eat this one"

I would wager that a solid half if not more of the economy exists under some sort of arrangement like that.

在 HN 中查看
bobthepanda 2026/04/15 23:49
是的，但通常还涉及验证电工是否确实遵守了专业规则，如果没有，他们就要承担责任
Right, but usually that also involves verifying that the electrician actually followed the professional rules, and if not, they have liability
在 HN 中查看
cucumber3732842 2026/04/16 00:23
那么法院会检查他们是否“只是执行命令”？

在我看来，服从命令实际上是一件神奇的事情，它会导致法院将责任从被告身上转移出去。

So the court checks if they were "just following orders"?

Sounds to me like following orders is in fact this magical thing that causes courts to direct liability away from the defendant.

在 HN 中查看
johngunderman 2026/04/16 04:06
我认为重点应该是“遵循限制其责任的做法和程序”=“根据其认证机构的规定进行尽职调查以降低风险”。

我们通常不会让人们对不可抗力或随机失败承担责任。例如，医疗事故诉讼通常需要证明医生故意玩忽职守。

现实生活中的一切都存在可量化的风险，我们为许多事情设立管理机构的部分原因是我们可以改进流程以降低风险。

这不仅仅是遵循命令:)它认识到风险的解决方案不是惩罚参与者而是改进系统。

I think the point is supposed to be that "following the practices and procedures that limit their liability" = "doing their due diligence to reduce risk in accordance with their credentialing body".

We generally don't hold people liable for acts of God or random chance failures. For example, malpractice suits generally need to prove that a doctor was intentionally negligent on their responsibility.

Everything in real life has quantifiable risk, and part of why we have governing bodies for many things is because we can improve our processes to reduce the risk.

It's not just following orders :) it's recognizing that the solution to risks isn't to punish the actor but to improve the system.

在 HN 中查看
cucumber3732842 2026/04/16 04:54
我并不是在考虑天灾类型的不可预见的事件，我在想“每个人都知道这是愚蠢和错误的，最终会导致问题，但遵守规则比挑战它更容易”的情况，因为“如果我遵守规则，我的屁股就被覆盖了”。
I'm not thinking act of god type unforseable event I'm thinking "everyone knows this is stupid and wrong and will cause a problem eventually but it's easier to just follow the rule than challenge it" situations because "if I follow the rules my ass is covered".
在 HN 中查看
g9yuayon 2026/04/16 05:20
> 工作中有多少是结构化的任务，而不是承担责任？

更准确地说，有多少工作是概率机械的。也就是说，有多少工作实际上是执行具有强先验的严肃贝叶斯决策。法学硕士非常擅长取代此类工作。

> How much of the job is a structured set of tasks vs. taking accountability?

More accurately, how many jobs are probabilistically mechanical. That is, how many jobs are really the execution of a serious Bayesian decisions with a strong prior. LLMs are really great at displacing such jobs.

在 HN 中查看
xienze 2026/04/15 23:38
> 这就是为什么我一直非常看好软件工程（但不看好编码）。您可以轻松地在人为或沙盒编码任务上设置 1) 和 2)，但随后 3) 会扩展并主导角色的其余部分。

为什么法学硕士和代理人不能比实际的软件工程师进一步进步以更好地完成这项软件工程工作？我从未见过有人对此给出满意的答案。尤其是关于犯错误的部分。很多针对 LLM 缺点（即生成蹩脚代码）的辩护都归结为“人类也会写出糟糕的代码”。好的？嗯，人类也会犯错误。从理论上讲，法学硕士软件工程师的收入将远远低于人类。那么我为什么要让你了解情况呢？

这就是为什么我无法理解那些对事情的发展方向感到头晕的软件工程师的心态。从理论上讲，您的专业知识确实没有什么是法学硕士无法实现的。

我们总是如此着迷于令人兴奋的新技术，以至于我们没有意识到负责人非常乐意将我们完全埋葬在它之中。

> Hence why I've been quite bullish on software engineering (but not coding). You can easy set up 1) and 2) on contrived or sandboxed coding tasks but then 3) expands and dominates the rest of the role.

Why can't LLMs and agents progress further to do this software engineering job better than an actual software engineer? I've never seen anyone give a satisfactory answer to this. Especially the part about making mistakes. A lot of the defense of LLM shortcomings (i.e., generating crappy code) comes down to "well humans write bad code too." OK? Well, humans make mistakes too. Theoretically, an LLM software engineer will make far fewer than a human. So why should I prefer keeping you in the loop?

It's why I just can't understand the mindset of software engineers who are giddy about the direction things are going. There really is nothing special about your expertise that an LLM can't achieve, theoretically.

We're always so enamored by new and exciting technology that we fail to realize the people in charge are more than happy to completely bury us with it.

在 HN 中查看
simplyluke 2026/04/16 01:56
谁比领域专家更适合试点法学硕士？

“软件工程师”这个职位涵盖了许多在获得法学硕士之前几年编写近乎零代码的人，至少在职业阶梯的较高级别是如此。在我看来，人们认为工作的唯一甚至主要功能就是输出代码，这表明他们对这个行业极度缺乏了解。在第一年或第二年之后，人们普遍认为代码是 简单的 工作的一部分。

Who is better positioned to pilot the LLM than a domain expert?

"Software engineer" as a job title has included a lot of people who write near-zero-code, at least at the higher levels of the career ladder, for years prior to LLMs. People assuming the only, or even primary, function of the job is outputting code reveal a profound lack of understanding of the industry in my opinion. Beyond the first year or two it has been commonly accepted that the code is the easy part of the job.

rootusrootus 2026/04/16 02:27
> 包括许多编写接近零代码的人，至少在职业阶梯的较高级别

我本以为 HN 读者对此非常熟悉。 LLM 可以让我的代码工作得更快或更高效，但对于 30 岁的我来说，我花了相当多的工作时间做任何事情 但 代码。

> has included a lot of people who write near-zero-code, at least at the higher levels of the career ladder

This is something that I would have thought HN readers were pretty familiar with. LLMs can make my code work faster or more prolific, but with 30yoe I spend a fairly significant chunk of my work time doing anything but code.

在 HN 中查看
Steppphennn 2026/04/15 23:54
> 为什么法学硕士和代理人不能比实际的软件工程师进一步进步以更好地完成这项软件工程工作？

因为机器永远无法承担责任。如果一名软件工程师一整年都在用创建较弱系统的提示来指导人工智能，那么被砍的是那个人，而不是人工智能。与另一位软件工程师相比，他指导提示扩展系统并产生额外的收入流。

> Why can't LLMs and agents progress further to do this software engineering job better than an actual software engineer?

Because a machine can never take accountability. If a software engineer throughout the entire year has been directing AI with prompts that created weaker systems then that person is on the chopping block, not the AI. Compared to another software engineer who directed prompts to expand the system and generate extra revenue streams.

xienze 2026/04/16 00:02
> 因为机器永远无法承担责任。

但商业领袖可以。

> 与另一位指导提示以扩展系统并产生额外收入流的软件工程师相比。

我认为你没有抓住要点。为什么法学硕士不能充分晋升为业务人员/产品经理所鼓励的真正的高级软件工程师，而不是你，一名软件工程师？如果法学硕士可以比你做得更好，为什么特别需要你呢？我不敢相信人们如此天真，看不到最终结果是什么：摆脱那些高级软件工程师，而高级管理层和经理们除了蔑视之外什么也没有。

> Because a machine can never take accountability.

A business leader can though.

> Compared to another software engineer who directed prompts to expand the system and generate extra revenue streams.

I think you're missing the point. Why can't an LLM advance sufficiently to be a REAL senior software engineer that a business person/product manager is prompting instead of YOU, a software engineer? Why are YOU specifically needed if an LLM can do a better job of it than you? I can't believe people are so naive to not see what the endgame is: getting rid of those primadonna software engineers that the C-suite and managers have nothing but contempt for.

rdevilla 2026/04/16 01:35

将论证驳回为“只是语义”是高中水平的论证。

ej88 2026/04/16 02:01

在 HN 中查看
ej88 2026/04/16 02:55
我想说还有更多的细微差别（免责声明：没有水晶球）

能够轻松从事业务工作的软件工程师——管理、与不同利益相关者合作、具有产品和设计品味、善于交际、推动业务成果将比以往任何时候都更受欢迎

同样，能够掌握技术、能够将模糊的想法分解为产品、利用代码进行原型设计以及与前任人员合作的业务主管也将具有极高的价值。

如果我是一名没有商业头脑的工程师或没有技术头脑的业务主管，我会担心（显然不包括首席执行官，但话又说回来，作为一名瑞典人创办自己的企业的障碍从未如此低过）

i would say theres more nuance than that (disclaimer: dont have a crystal ball)

software engineers who are comfortable doing business work - managing, working with different stakeholders, having product and design taste, being sociable, driving business outcomes are going to be more desired than ever

likewise, business leads who can be technical, can decompose vague ideas into product, leverage code to prototype and work with the previous person will also be extremely high value.

i would be concerned if i was an engineer with no business acumen or a business lead with no technical acumen (not counting CEOs obviously, but then again the barrier to starting your own business as a SWE has never been lower)

nradov 2026/04/16 01:50
有趣的是，这就是 COBOL 最初于 1960 年开发的原因：以便业务人员可以自己编写软件，而不需要软件工程师。在某种程度上，这确实有效。历史重演。
It's funny, that's why COBOL was originally developed in 1960: so that business people could write software themselves without needing software engineers. And it sort of worked, to an extent. History repeats itself.

nradov 2026/04/16 01:53

在 HN 中查看
ej88 2026/04/16 01:05
问题不在于他们是否会犯错误（他们确实犯了错误！尽管错误的确切定义是微妙的），而在于如果软件出现故障并造成数百万人损失或人员死亡，他们是否能够承担责任。软件工程师的大部分报酬是为了对他们的工作负责。如果“商人”指示他们的代理人构建一些软件并承担责任——恭喜！他们现在也是软件工程师:)

软件工程师/业务人员/产品/设计与其他一切之间的界限将变得模糊，因为人工智能增加了个人的影响力。我认为这个新世界将会有更多的“软件工程师”，但也会有更多的产品人员、更多的业务人员、更多的公司。

It's not about whether they make mistakes (they do! although the exact definition of a mistake is nuanced), but whether they can take accountability if the software fails and millions are lost or people die. A large part of the premium paid on software engineers is to take accountability for their work. If a "business person" directs their agent to build some software and takes accountability -- congrats! They are also now a software engineer :)

The lines between a software engineer / business person / product / design and everything else will blur, because AI increases the individual person's leverage. I posit that there will be more 'software engineers' in this new world, but also more product people, more business people, more companies in general.

icedchai 2026/04/16 06:21
许多经验丰富的软件工程师将转入基础架构或架构师角色（如果他们还没有的话）。经验丰富的工程师最适合使用法学硕士，因为他们可以验证输出实际上是否正确，而不仅仅是看起来有效。新来的人将会处于一个糟糕的境地。
Many experienced software engineers will move into infrastructure or architect roles, if they haven't already. Experienced engineers are in the best position to use LLMs because they can validate the output as actually being correct, not just looking like it works. Newer folks are going to be in a bad spot.

monknomo 2026/04/16 04:45
我认为，乐观的说法是，软件开发人员作为职业会消亡，就像系统管理员一样。 但就像开发运营一样，将会出现一个新的待命名角色（或一组角色）
The optimistic spin is, I think, software developer as a career dies, just like sysadmin. But just like dev-ops, a new to-be-named role (or set of roles) will arise

xienze 2026/04/15 23:42
> 领域专家就没问题

但我不明白这如何经得起哪怕是最轻微的审查。我们实际上是在培训法学硕士成为领域专家。

> domain experts will be fine

But I don't see how this holds up to even the slightest amount of scrutiny. We're literally training LLMs to BE domain experts.

在 HN 中查看
bwestergard 2026/04/15 23:55
我认为这些争论往往会陷入僵局，因为人们倾向于以下两种观点之一：

1) 我在法学硕士方面的经历非常令人印象深刻，我认为他们的产出通常比典型的开发人员产出的要好。看不到这一点的人没有获得足够的经验来使用我认为令人印象深刻的模型，或者否认他们的技能贬值。

2）我的法学硕士经历很平凡。那些认为它们具有变革性的人缺乏区分平庸和优秀代码所需的专业知识，导致他们否认两者之间存在差异。

I think these arguments tend to reach impasse because one gravitates to one of two views:

1) My experiences with LLMs are so impressive that I consider their output to generally be better than what the typical developer would produce. People who can't see this have not gotten enough experience with the models I find so impressive, or are in denial about the devaluation of their skills.

2) My experiences with LLMs have been mundane. People who see them as transformative lack the expertise required to distinguish between mediocre and excellent code, leading them to deny there is a difference.

在 HN 中查看
xienze 2026/04/16 00:04
不确定这就是我的意思。第二阵营的人认为法学硕士无法取代真正的软件工程师的工作。

我想知道的是1号营地的人。他们相信法学硕士可以完成任何事情，并且比任何人都更好地理解代码库（情况可能就是这样！）。然而，他们同时确信，由于“原因”，他们仍然需要进行提示。

Not sure that's what I was getting at. People in camp 2 don't think an LLM can take over the job of a real software engineer.

It's people in camp 1 that I wonder about. They're convinced that LLMs can accomplish anything and understand a codebase better than anyone (and that may be the case!). However, they're simultaneously convinced that they'll still be needed to do the prompting because ???reasons???.

在 HN 中查看
TheGRS 2026/04/16 02:04
一种解释是，有些人认为我们可能已经达到了法学硕士合理范围内的极限。任何工作的很多职能都不容易转化为法学硕士，更多的是与人互动或批判性思维，这是法学硕士无法做到的。我不确定这是否是每个人的理由，但这是我个人对这种情况的看法。就像工作会发生变化一样，但我们很可能不会因为人工智能而彻底失去它们。
One explanation is that some think we might be getting to the limits of what an LLM can reasonably do. There's a lot of functions of any job that are not easily translated to an LLM and are much more about interacting with people or critical thinking in a way LLMs can't do. I'm not sure if that's everyone's rationale but that's my personal view of the situation. Like the jobs will change but we likely won't be losing them to AI outright.
在 HN 中查看
paleotrope 2026/04/16 00:53
我今天在想，我需要转向制造和销售铲子，但另一个问题是未来任何人都需要铲子。
I was thinking today that I need to pivot to making and selling shovels, but then other issue is is anyone going to need shovels in the future.
在 HN 中查看
georgemcbay 2026/04/16 00:30
直到去年年底，我一直处于 2) 水平，然后法学硕士/代理人/安全带的能力跳跃并没有让我完全达到 1)，但在这个方向上已经有了足够大的跳跃，我不明白为什么我不应该相信我们很快就能到达那里。

所以现在我倾向于认为很多人都严重否认法学硕士在毕业之前就不会变得更好了。 他们 我个人最终陷入了压路机之下，但我不确定这种信仰是基于什么。

我还认为人们倾向于以过于二元的方式对待“法学硕士是否会取代<工作>”问题。 法学硕士不必取代每一个从事特定工作的人，从而产生巨大的破坏性，如果他们通过提高最后 10% 的工作效率来取代 90% 从事特定工作的人，从经济角度来看，这仍然是一个灾难性的工作岗位流失。

即使他们只取代 10-30%，这仍然是一个巨大的流离失所，作为参考，大萧条期间的失业率为 25%。

I was at 2) until the end of last year, then LLM/agent/harnesses had a capability jump that didn't quite bring me to be a 1) but was a big enough jump in that direction that I don't see why I shouldn't believe we get there soonish.

So now I tend to think a lot of people are in heavy denial in thinking that LLMs are going to stop getting better before they personally end up under the steamroller, but I'm not sure what this faith is based on.

I also think people tend to treat the "will LLMs replace <job>" question in too much of a binary manner. LLMs don't have to replace every last person that does a specific job to be wildly disruptive, if they replace 90% of the people that do a particular job by making the last 10% much more productive that's still a cataclysmic amount of job displacement in economic terms.

Even if they replace just 10-30% that's still a huge amount of displacement, for reference the unemployment rate during the Great Depression was 25%.

在 HN 中查看
jandrewrogers 2026/04/16 01:04
法学硕士无法理解大量的领域专业知识。他们依赖通过别人的写作来获​​取知识是一个真正的限制。许多人类领域的专业知识并不是通过这种方式获得的。

在从第一原理掌握某个领域之前，他们还有很长的路要走，这限制了掌握的可能性。

An enormous amount of domain expertise is not legible to LLMs. Their dependence on obtaining knowledge through someone else's writing is a real limitation. A lot of human domain expertise is not acquired that way.

They still have a long way to go before they can master a domain from first principles, which constrains the mastery possible.

在 HN 中查看
vharuck 2026/04/16 01:52
人们在接受法学硕士的速记术语时需要小心。他们不像我们那样学习。在最低级别，他们预测哪些标记遵循标记主体。这使他们能够以一种非常有用的方式模拟知识。这类似于用户活动的时间序列模型：时间序列模型不会密切关注用户何时活跃，它没有阅读有关用户行为的研究，它只是反映了数据点之间的数学关系。

对于法学硕士和这种“模糊”的领域专业知识来说，即使法学硕士的培训材料都不包含某些智慧，如果材料包含足够的问题案例和领域专家提供的解决方案，我们应该期望模型能够在它们之间找到良好的关系。法学硕士从未摄取过明确的推理文档是无关紧要的，因为它不执行推理。

People need to be careful about buying into the shorthand lingo with LLMs. They do not learn like we do. At the lowest level, they predict which tokens follow a body of tokens. This lets them emulate knowledge in a very useful way. This is similar to a time series model of user activity: the time series model does not keep tabs on users to see when they are active, it has not read studies about user behavior, it just reflects a mathematical relationship between points of data.

For an LLM and this "vague" domain expertise, even if none of the LLM's training material includes certain nuggets of wisdom, if the material includes enough cases of problems and the solutions offered by domain experts, we should expect the model to find a decent relationship between them. That the LLM has never ingested an explicit documentation of the reasoning is irrelevant, because it does not perform reasoning.

在 HN 中查看
jandrewrogers 2026/04/16 03:09
我所指的领域专业知识并不含糊，它实际上并不作为训练数据存在。没有与最新技术相关的问题和解决方案案例可供研究。在某些情况下，这是法学硕士早在到达现场之前就有意和设计的（例如商业秘密、国家安全等）。

我们甚至在计算机科学中有一些臭名昭著的“黑暗”领域，人类几乎不可能到达这些领域的前沿，因为支撑许多最先进技术的研究几十年来一直没有作为公共文献存在。如果你想学习它，你要么必须认识愿意帮助你的领域专家，要么从第一原理重新发明它。

The domain expertise I'm referring to isn't vague, it literally doesn't exist as training data. There are no cases of problems and solutions to study that are relevant to the state-of-the-art. In some cases this is by intent and design (e.g. trade secrets, national security, etc) long before for LLMs arrived on the scene.

We even have some infamous "dark" domains in computer science where it is nearly impossible for a human to get to the frontier because the research that underpins much of the state-of-the-art hasn't existed as public literature for decades. If you want to learn it, you either have to know a domain expert willing to help you or reinvent it from first principles.

在 HN 中查看
bauerd 2026/04/16 01:37
>在从第一原理掌握某个领域之前，他们还有很长的路要走，这限制了掌握的可能性。

精通是没有必要的。为什么 Waymos 缺乏司机？并不是因为自动驾驶汽车已经掌握了驾驶技术，而是因为自动驾驶的效果足够好，以至于出租车司机无法从中获得经济效益。

>They still have a long way to go before they can master a domain from first principles, which constrains the mastery possible.

Mastery isn't necessary. Why are Waymos lacking drivers? Not because self-driving cars have mastered driving, but because self-driving works sufficiently well that the economics don't play out for the cab driver.

mrdependable 2026/04/16 00:22
我认为人工智能不会取代首席执行官或高管层中的任何人的原因是非常明显的。他们将自己视为公司。其他人都是资源。人工智能正在取代资源，就像投资一台全新的割草机一样。对他们来说，用人工智能取代高管就像说你要嫁给一把扫帚。
I think the reason AI isn't going to replace CEOs, or anyone in the C suite, is pretty obvious. They see themselves as the company. Everyone else is a resource. AI is here to replace resources, just like investing in a brand new lawn mower. For them, replacing an executive with AI is like saying you're going to marry a broom.
在 HN 中查看
torben-friis 2026/04/16 02:10
大多数公司都包含高层也不知道的多层业务背景。

从“未付账单都是这样处理的”到“网站有一个需要每年更新的证书”到“我们需要在印度尼西亚每两周报告一次收入，并且我们需要在表格生效之前重试几次表格”。

这对于法学硕士来说从根本上来说是不可能做到的，因为高层不知道要要求什么，而且如果他们这样做了，那么无论人工智能如何辅助，将所有事情都放在一个人的头脑中是不可行的。

因此，我认为仅此一点就可以保证大量的工作岗位不会被取代。

Most companies contain several layers worth of business context that the higher ups have no idea of, as well.

Everything from "unpaid bills are handled this way" through "the website has a certificate that needs yearly renewal" to "we need to report our earnings biweekly in Indonesia, and we need to retry the form several times before it works".

This is not fundamentally doable by LLMs because the higher ups wouldn't know what to ask for, and if they did it would not be feasible to keep everything in single persons head, no matter how AI-assisted.

So that alone I think guarantees a good amount of unreplaced jobs.

在 HN 中查看
pixl97 2026/04/16 02:37
呃，这听起来像是很多公司已经被替换的人了。

>“该网站有证书需要每年更新”

例如，为什么现代证书的时间被缩短到很短。推动一切自动化。

>“未付账单按此处理”

任何有类似情况的公司都会开始改变他们的流程。

你有这样的想法（也许人工智能公司的首席执行官也有这样的想法），这将就像一个容器投放进来。不，相反，这些东西将成为渐进过程变化的一部分，而人类部分最终将消失。

>因此，我认为仅此一点就可以保证大量未被取代的工作岗位。

百分之几。归根结底，这才是最重要的。在一个习惯了低失业率的社会中，一旦所有年龄段的失业率都达到很高水平，事情就会变得糟糕。在有社会保障网和就业计划的地方，变坏可能需要更长的时间，但在像美国这样的共产主义国家，所以预计富人会向穷人开枪。

Eh, this sounds like the people that have been replaced at a lot of companies already.

>"the website has a certificate that needs yearly renewal"

For example, why modern certificates are being dropped down to very short times. The push to automate everything.

>"unpaid bills are handled this way"

Any company that has things like this will start changing their process.

You have this idea (and maybe the AI company CEOs do too) that this will be like a container drop in. No, instead these things will be part of a gradual process change and human parts will eventually disappear.

>So that alone I think guarantees a good amount of unreplaced jobs.

What percent. This is what matters at the end of the day. Once unemployment reaches high rates over all age groups in a society that is used to low unemployment things go bad. It can take longer to go bad where you have social safety nets and jobs programs, but in places like the US that is communism, so expect the haves to shoot at the have nots.

janalsncm 2026/04/16 03:07
> 他们将自己视为公司。其他人都是资源

由于对这些事情如何运作一无所知，我想知道董事会是否也会以同样的方式看待它？即使在今天，我也能看到以下的结果：

CEO说X。董事会成员当场将一堆战略信息放入ChatGPT，这使得Y比X更有说服力。

在那一刻，首席执行官会发现自己正在与聊天机器人争论，聊天机器人可能会比你说“变革”这个词更快地说出看似合理的废话。

也许他们今天赢得了争论，但最终首席执行官将在职能上被替换，并最终实际上被替换或淡化。

> They see themselves as the company. Everyone else is a resource

Knowing nothing about how these things work, I wonder if the board will see it the same way? Even today I could see the following play out:

CEO says X. Board member puts a bunch of strategic info into ChatGPT on the spot which argues Y more convincingly than X.

In that moment, the CEO will find themselves arguing against a chatbot, which can gish gallop with plausible bs faster than you can say the word “transformative”.

Maybe they win the argument today, but eventually the CEO will be functionally replaced, and eventually actually replaced or watered down.

siliconc0w 2026/04/15 23:00
人工智能的问题在于它不像以前的任何技术。 可能会有临时工作来填补空缺，但它们不会成为职业。 人工智能将进行流程工程和自我优化。 提示巫术是一个很好的例子，因为今天它完全没有必要，并且实际上不会提高性能，并且他们将继续使引导/操纵模型变得更容易。

我们实际上是在试图建立一种智能来取代我们。

The problem with AI is that it isn't like any previous technology. There may be temporary jobs to fill in the gaps but they won't be careers. The AI will do the process engineering and self optimization. The prompt witchcraft is a good example because today its totally unnecessary and doesn't actually increase performance, and they'll continue to make it easier to direct/steer the models.

We're literally trying to build an intelligence to replace us.

pardon_me 2026/04/16 01:13
人类物种。 “我们”并不包括所有人，也不一定意味着该过程是通过协作和计划（共谋）发生的。自动化竞赛正在如期进行；不受任何团体控制并受竞争约束。博弈论表明，如果我们做到了这一点，最终的结果就是我们被取代。 “我们”作为一个物种，是让这一切发生的人。
The human species. "We" doesn't include everyone and doesn't necessarily imply the process happens through collaboration and planning (conspiracy). The race to automation is happening as expected; outside any group control and bound by competition. Game theory suggests the end result is us being replaced, if we make it that far. "We" as a species are the ones making it happen.

simonw 2026/04/15 23:05

在 HN 中查看
giaour 2026/04/16 00:26
Data & Society 早在 2019 年就发表了一篇关于这一角色的论文，但使用了“道德溃缩区”一词，因为他们专注于如何在自动驾驶汽车事故中分配责任： https://www.researchgate.net/publication/351054898_Moral_Cru...

不过，“肉盾”的物理性很好

Data & Society put out a paper on this role back in 2019 but used the term "moral crumple zones" since they were focusing on how to assign blame in autonomous vehicle crashes: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/351054898_Moral_Cru...

"Meat shields" has a nice physicality to it, though

aphyr 2026/04/16 00:46

Thank you for this--I remember reading this paper when it came out, but forgot it by the time I wrote this section. Will add a citation.
在 HN 中查看
paulnpace 2026/04/16 01:59
谢谢，我还没有找到这个。

我考虑的是，就自动驾驶汽车而言，没有人需要承担刑事责任，即使我们其他人如果犯了一些可怕的错误，可能会因疏忽而承担刑事责任。从哲学上讲，有某种原因表明犯罪行为需要受到的惩罚不仅仅是经济责任（例如监禁），而自动驾驶汽车则不受此处罚。目前，自动驾驶汽车也不受实际道路法律的约束，因为警察无权对自动驾驶汽车执行任何强制措施。

Thank you, I had not found this one.

What I had considered is that in the case of self-driving cars, nobody is criminally accountable, even though the rest of us may be criminally negligent should we make some horrific error. Philosophically, there is some kind of reason that criminal acts require punishment beyond mere financial liability (e.g., prison time) and self-driving cars are exempted from this. Currently, self-driving cars are also exempt from the actual laws of the road because the police are dis-empowered to enforce anything on the self-driving car.

在 HN 中查看
nlawalker 2026/04/16 08:56
这是我思考最多的人工智能方面。我想知道我们是否会达到这样的地步：它是否会促使我们认真思考“责任”到底是什么，以及它提供什么价值（以及为谁提供），特别是在标准就业情况下，员工在犯错时通常无法“使雇主完整”，实际上只能因此而受到“惩罚”。
This is the aspect of AI I ponder the most. I wonder if we’ll get to the point where it prompts a real hard think about what “accountability” really is, and what value it provides (and to whom), particularly in the standard employment situation where an employee generally can’t “make their employer whole” in the event of a mistake and can really only be “punished” for it.
在 HN 中查看
buildbot 2026/04/15 23:24
这确实符合逻辑；使用该工具的人最终要承担责任。

在我看来，该工具是否太强大或不道德而无法使用是一个正交讨论。极端地说，核武器仍然需要有人发射或投掷。 （我们仍然应该经常讨论安全和道德问题！）

It just makes logical sense really; the human using the tool is in the end responsible.

Whether the tool is too powerful or ethical to use is an orthogonal discussion, in my opinion. Taken to the extreme, nuclear weapons still need someone fire or drop them. (We should still have discussions on safety and ethics always!)

chii 2026/04/15 23:38

why can't the name be 'scape goat'? Since that's what they are - the "real" responsibility rests on the owners, and they happily shed it as limited liability ownership of shares.

在 HN 中查看
ai_critic 2026/04/15 22:29
我认为这是分类学上的一次有趣的尝试，但它有点神奇的思维末端（我这么说是因为有人做了很多被描述为 咒语 角色）。它是作者之前的巫术美学（参见他出色的“<x>技术面试”系列）和进步的劳工政治（在当前的自动化推动中渐近注定会失败）的结合。

我认为，想象力的最大失败是假设我们会使用人类来完成大多数（或*任何）这些工作——例如，haruspex的工作最好留给可以处理无数内部状态的法学硕士（这是机械解释领域）。

I think that this is an interesting attempt at taxonomy, but it's a bit on the magical thinking end (and I say this as somebody that does a good amount of what's described as the incanter role). It's a combination of the author's previous witchy aesthetic (see his excellent "<x>ing the technical interview" series) and progressive labor politics (which are asymptotically doomed in the current automation push).

The biggest failure of imagination, I think, is the assumption we'd use humans for most (or *any) of these jobs--for example, the work of the haruspex is better left to an LLM that can process the myriad of internal states (this is the mechanical interpretation field).

在 HN 中查看
mitthrowaway2 2026/04/15 23:08
是的，我也有同样的印象。我很同情作者的观点，但我什至无法鼓起他们在这里表现出的最低限度的乐观情绪。所描述的“工艺工程师”本身很快就会被自动化系统取代。我认为，“统计工程师”永远无法跟上人工智能模型的变化速度，每次更新都可能在每种语言/上下文等中产生不同的统计行为和偏差，因此不太可能有人愿意付钱给他们来开发需要深厚专业知识的东西。更有可能的是，这项工作将在人工智能基础模型公司完成——但只会完成一次，然后纳入培训过程。
Yes, I had the same impression. I'm sympathetic to the author's perspective but I can't muster even the minimal optimism they've shown here. The "process engineers" as described would themselves quickly be replaced by an automated system. The "statistical engineers", I think, would never be able to keep up with the rate of change of the AI models, which would likely have different statistical behavior and biases in each language/context/etc with each update, and so it's unlikely anyone would pay them to develop that required deep expertise in the first place. More likely, that work would be done at an AI foundation model company -- but it would be done just once, and then incorporated into the training process.

在 HN 中查看
Aperocky 2026/04/15 22:44
作为一名工程师，我对这份工作从未如此兴奋。

我的实现速度和修复我键入的代码的错误是瓶颈 - 现在我只考虑一个实现，然后它就存在 - 只要我正确地考虑了结构/输入/输出/可测试性和逻辑流程并确保我包含了所有这些信息，它就可以通过测试很好地工作。

Unix 哲学也适用于法学硕士 - 你可以拥有可以做好一件事并且只能做好一件事的软件，该软件适合其上下文并且不会导致随意的行为。

现在，我的一天基本上围绕着交付/改进交付集中的工程思维，在我看来，这是关于工程师职业本身的纯粹部分。我非常喜欢它。

As an engineer, I'm never more excited about this job.

My implementation speed and bug fixing my typed code to be the bottleneck - now I just think about an implementation and it then exist - As long as I thought about the structure/input/output/testability and logic flow correctly and made sure I included all that information, it just works, nicely, with tests.

Unix philosophy works well with LLM too - you can have software that does one thing well and only one thing well, that fit in their context and do not lead to haphazard behavior.

Now my day essentially revolves around delivering/improving on delivering concentrated engineering thinking, which in my opinion is the pure part about engineer profession itself. I like it quite a lot.

在 HN 中查看
hombre_fatal 2026/04/15 22:52
我基本上同意你的观点。

尽管我有点怀念的是在构建我自己的软件时使用它来对迄今为止的抽象有一个发自内心的感觉。我发现可测试性是“好用”的一个足够好的代理，因为我认为“好用”往往意味着子系统足够解耦以覆盖意外的使用模式，这是可测试性的一个偶然的副作用。

我担心的一个问题是展示能力变得越来越困难。

例如Github 的个人资料是一个很好的信号，尽管没有人关心，除非招聘者是一位可以评估它的工程师。但现在这个信号更加垃圾。甚至自述文件和博客文章也变得更糟糕，因为它们不一定再展示您自己的沟通技巧或您如何看待问题。

I mostly agree with you.

Though something I half-miss is using my own software as I build it to get a visceral feel for the abstractions so far. I've found that testability is a good enough proxy for "nice to use" since I think "nice to use" tends to mean that a subsystem is decoupled enough to cover unexpected usage patterns, and that's an incidental side-effect of testability.

One concern I have is that it's getting harder to demonstrate ability.

e.g. Github profiles were a good signal though one that nobody cared about unless the hiring person was an engineer who could evaluate it. But now that signal is even more rubbish. Even readmes and blog posts are becoming worse signals since they don't necessarily showcase your own communication skills anymore nor how you think about problems.

在 HN 中查看
Aperocky 2026/04/15 22:59
有趣的是，我认为 github 和交流仍然是我所看到的重要组成部分。

Github 代码本身可能无关紧要，但产品是 KISS/UNIX 吗？或者这是否表明完全缺乏关于应该添加哪些“功能”的纪律。如果你看到某个东西有多个弱或完全不相关的特征串在一起，那么它就说明了一些事情。此外，人工智能通常会创建意大利面结构，并需要人类牧养以确保结构保持完好。

沟通也一样。我有人工智能的气味，我知道是否有什么东西是人工智能的。在我目前的工作中，发送的文档总是以期望其他人阅读的方式开头——这一部分 100% 由 aperocky 输入——并且我省去了语法和拼写检查以增加真实性。然后我将添加——以下部分是人工智能生成的——以标记我个人写作的结束。

我认为这是未来的发展方向。我将有意思考传递给人工智能，而不是相反。肯定有知识回流，但只有人类拥有意图，至少目前如此。

Funny enough, I think github and communication are still a huge part of what I see.

Github code itself maybe irrelevant, but is the product KISS/UNIX? Or is it an demonstration of complete lack of discipline about what "feature" should be added. If you see something that have multiple weakly or completely irrelevant feature strung together, it's saying something. Additionally, AI would often create speghetti structures, and require human shepherding to ensure the structure remain sound.

Same with communication. I have AI smell, I know if something is AI slop. In my current job, docs sent with expectation for others to read always prefaced with -- this section typed 100% by aperocky -- and I dispensed with grammar and spelling checks for added authenticity. I'll then add -- following section is AI generated -- to mark the end of my personal writing.

I think that is the way to go in the future. I pass intentional thinking into AI, not the other way around. There are knowledge flowing back for sure, but only humans possess intention, at least for now.

the_af 2026/04/15 23:19
> 但现在这个信号更加垃圾。甚至自述文件和博客文章也变得更糟糕，因为它们不一定再展示您自己的沟通技巧或您如何看待问题。

是的。我发现一些前同事在 AWS 工作并用完整的人工智能语言撰写英语技术博客文章，据我所知，他们几乎无法用母语写作，更不用说英语了。充满了常见的“这不是 X，这是 Y”，充满了人工智能的废话。大部分文字都是填充内容，偶尔有一些真实内容的花絮。

我以前不知道，但现在博客文章已经变得噪音多于信号。

> But now that signal is even more rubbish. Even readmes and blog posts are becoming worse signals since they don't necessarily showcase your own communication skills anymore nor how you think about problems.

Yup. I've spotted former coworkers who I know for a fact can barely write in their native language, let alone in English, working for AWS and writing English-language technical blog posts in full AI-ese. Full of the usual "it's not X, it's Y", full of AI-slop. Most of the text is filler, with a few tidbits of real content here and there.

I don't know before, but now blog posts have become more noise than signal.

the_af 2026/04/16 03:39
> 在 LinkedIn 上尤其糟糕

同意，但公平地说，LinkedIn 一开始就特别糟糕。

甚至在人工智能出现之前，LinkedIn 上的帖子就受到了嘲笑。自鸣得意或自怜，充满空洞的陈词滥调和“经验教训”和“旅程”（结束或开始）。从一开始就没有什么值得一读的东西。

现在情况当然更糟了。我认为我无法忍受在 LinkedIn 上读到另一位自封的专家写的关于他们完全没有根据的策略和/或教训和/或对人工智能的怀疑。

我只上 LinkedIn 来解决日常难题！

> It's especially bad on LinkedIn

Agreed, but to be fair, LinkedIn was especially bad to begin with.

Even before AI-slop, LinkedIn posts were rightfully mocked. Self-congratulatory or self-pitying, full of empty platitudes and "lessons learned" and "journeys" (ended or started). There was never anything worth reading to begin with.

Now it's of course worse. I don't think I can stand reading about another self-appointed expert on LinkedIn writing about their completely unwarranted strategy and/or lessons and/or skepticism about AI.

I only go to LinkedIn for the daily puzzles!

在 HN 中查看
simonw 2026/04/15 23:07
有趣的是，我一直观察到担任高级工程师、领导和工程管理职位的同事生成的代码量显着增加。

他们现在可以利用 20 多年的经验在会议间隙构建工作系统。以前，他们必须腾出至少半天不间断的时间才能完成一些有意义的事情。

Anecdotally I've been observing a significant uptick in the amount of code being produced by my peers who are in senior engineer, leadership and engineering management positions.

They can take their 20+ years of experience and use it to build working systems in the gaps between meetings now. Previously they would have to carve out at least half a day of uninterrupted time to get something meaningful done.

在 HN 中查看
rootusrootus 2026/04/16 02:31
> 现在就在会议间隙建立工作系统

同意，我确实做到了这一点。 坐在一个会议上，有人询问我们可以构建什么工具，它可能有什么功能，他们的选择是什么。 因此，当我们聊天时，我让 Claude 构建了一个工作演示。

最后，它仍然需要变成一个企业应用程序，并附带所有恼人的装备，但对于演示工作来说，它是惊人的。

> build working systems in the gaps between meetings now

Agreed, I've actually done this. Sitting in a meeting where someone was asking about what tooling we could build, what it might be capable of, what their options were. So while we were chatting I was having Claude build a working demo.

In the end it still needs to be turned into an enterprise app with all the annoying accoutrements that go with that, but for demo work it was phenomenal.

eloisant 2026/04/16 01:44
我既兴奋又害怕。

是的，我的工作效率比以前提高了很多，而且我确信我们无法完全摆脱工程师……但是，我的 5 人团队多久才会被一名工程师取代呢？我会成为保住工作的人还是被解雇的 4 人之一？

I'm excited and scared at the same time.

Yes I'm much more productive than before, and I'm convinced we can't get rid of engineers altogether... But how long until my team of 5 gets replaced by a single engineer? Am I going to be the one to keep my job or one of the 4 to be let go?

在 HN 中查看
Aperocky 2026/04/16 03:08
如果团队做完全相同的事情，时间不会太长。

知道要构建什么和不构建什么的能力与知道如何构建它同样重要。我仍然认为工程师在这方面有优势。我童年时所有关于我应该能够做什么或建造什么的梦想都已成为现实，唯一阻碍我的是缺乏时间。 我还想走得更快

If the team does the exact same thing, not very long.

The ability to know what to build and what not to build is going to be as important as knowing how to build it. I still think engineers have an edge here. All my childhood dreams of what I should be able to do or build are coming to a reality and the only thing that is blocking me is lack of time. I want to go faster still

在 HN 中查看
at-fates-hands 2026/04/16 01:58
十年前，当我从事自动化行业时，他们一直告诉我们永远不要告诉人们这将取代他们。你告诉他们的是，这将使他们的团队最终能够专注于什么 真的 很重要。现在，他们可以专注于更大的问题，而不是处理所有这些重复性任务。每个人都认同，团队都觉得我们正在做的自动化确实会让他们的工作变得更轻松。

但从来没有。

经理们意识到，在我们完成后，他们可以精简团队，事实上，裁员了数百人。用更少的人做同样的工作对他们来说是有利的，因为现在他们因为增加了公司的利润而获得了更多的奖金和加薪。许多经理除了裁员一半之外什么也没做，却得到了更快的晋升。

所以，是的，要害怕，非常害怕，并制定 B 计划和 C 计划。创造这个产品的人对于它将如何彻底改变商业有着乐观的看法。实际的企业主和首席执行官只是看到了另一种减少人力资本以增加利润的新方法。

When I was in automation a decade ago, they keep telling us to never tell people this is going to replace them. What you tell them is it will allow their teams to finally focus on what really matters. Instead of working on all these repetitive tasks, now they can focus on the much larger issues. Everybody bought in, teams felt like the automation we were doing was really going to make their jobs easier.

It never did.

Managers realized they could trim their teams down after we were done and did in fact, layoff people by the hundreds. Doing the same work with less people was beneficial to them because now they got bigger bonuses and salary increases for adding to the bottom line of the company. Many managers who did nothing more than layoff half their team were promoted faster up the ranks.

So yes, be scared, be VERY scared and have a Plan B and a Plan C going forward. The people who created this have rose colored glasses on how its going to revolutionize business. The actual businesses owners and CEO's just a see another new way to reduce human capital in order to increase profits.

在 HN 中查看
the_af 2026/04/15 23:22
> 作为一名工程师，我对这份工作从未如此兴奋。

您认为人工智能趋势需要多长时间才能使您仍然感到兴奋的工作部分实现大部分自动化？

每个人都认为不会是他们，会是 其他的 将会受到影响。我们都想什么 我们做 它在某种程度上是独一无二的，无法被人工智能自动化取代，而且我们的工作暂时是安全的。

> As an engineer, I'm never more excited about this job.

How long do you think it'll take for the AI trend to mostly automate the parts of your job that still make you excited?

Everyone thinks it won't be them, it will be others that will be impacted. We all think what we do is somehow unique and cannot be automated away by AI, and that our jobs are safe for the time being.

在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/16 04:01
> 您认为人工智能趋势需要多长时间才能使您仍然感到兴奋的工作部分实现大部分自动化？

这项工作最令人兴奋的部分始终是，聆听闲聊，从中发现人们在生活中遇到困难的微妙线索，然后解决这些问题。我认为今天人工智能已经可以很好地处理这个问题，除了：

你必须说服人们，尤其是非技术人员，与机器而不是人类进行闲聊

-或者-

说服他们并让机器始终监听他们与人类的闲聊

在当前的社会格局中，这些都不是那么令人愉快。如果说有什么不同的话，那就是人们似乎越来越厌倦让技术进入他们的思想。也许未来人类永远不会接受机器总是在那里试图找出它们出了什么问题。

人工智能取代工作的问题在于，很多工作的存在只是因为人们想要与其他人交谈并愿意为公司付钱。

> How long do you think it'll take for the AI trend to mostly automate the parts of your job that still make you excited?

The exciting part of the job is, and always has been, listening to idle chitchat where you pick up on the subtle cues of where one is finding difficulty in their life and then solving those problems. I think AI could already largely handle that today just fine, except:

You have to convince, especially non-technical, people to have idle chitchat with machines instead of humans

-or-

Convince them of and into having a machine always listening in to their idle conversations with humans

Neither of those are all that palatable in the current social landscape. If anything, people seem to be growing more weary of letting technology into their thoughts. Maybe there is never a future where humans become accepting of machines being always there trying to figure out what is wrong with them.

The trouble with AI replacing jobs is that a lot of jobs exist only because people want to have other people to talk to and are willing to pay for the company.

在 HN 中查看
Aperocky 2026/04/16 00:39
作为代币使用率处于第 99% 的人，我非常清楚代理无法取代我的判断，有两个方面：

1.如果超出了上下文，代理就会做随机的事情，这通常违背简单性和连贯的逻辑结构。

2. LLM 零意图，依靠你来决定构建什么，更重要的是不构建。

因此，我是同时工作的代理数量的限制，因为我的工程判断输出仍然有限。我在做出判断和做出判断方面确实变得更好了。超过这个限制，输出就变成垃圾。

在今年和今天，人工智能无论如何都不会自动化我，我有一些他们根本没有的东西。

As someone in 99th percentile in terms of token usage, it's super clear to me where the agent will not be able to replace my judgement, two areas:

1. if it exceed the context the agent does random stuff, that are often against simplicity and coherent logical structure.

2. LLM has zero intention, and rely on you to decide what to build and more importantly not build.

As such, I'm the limit of the numbers of concurrent agents working fo rme, because there is still a limit to my output of engineering judgement. I do get better, both at generating and delivering this judgement. Exceeding this limit, the output becomes garbage.

At this current year and date, the AI does not automate me in anyway, I have something that they just flat out don't have.

在 HN 中查看
the_af 2026/04/16 00:56
我在这里唱反调，不是在对抗你，而是在大声思考。

> 如果它超出了上下文，代理就会做随机的事情，这通常违背简单性和连贯的逻辑结构。

这是当前的技术限制。您确定它不会在不久/中期的将来被克服吗？

> LLM 的意图为零，依靠您来决定构建什么，更重要的是不构建

但我们正在努力消除或自动化这一层，对吗？这可能是夸张的（事实上，它 是）但是 Anthropic 等人不是预测到了这一点吗？为什么你的老板或你老板的老板不代替你这样做呢？如果他们目前缺乏判断力，如果他们不必浪费时间学习如何编码，你确定他们无法获得判断力吗？如果不是现在，那么很快呢？

> 在今年和今天，人工智能无论如何都不会自动化我

不是 现在， 的确。但很快呢？换句话说，你不应该既担心又兴奋吗？

Playing devil's advocate here, I'm not antagonizing you but thinking out loud.

> if it exceed the context the agent does random stuff, that are often against simplicity and coherent logical structure.

That's a current technical limitation. Are you so sure it won't be overcome in the near/mid future?

> LLM has zero intention, and rely on you to decide what to build and more importantly not build

But work is being done to even remove or automate this layer, right? It can be hyperbole (in fact, it is) but aren't Anthropic et al predicting this? Why wouldn't your boss, or your boss' boss, do this instead of you? If they lack the judgment currently, are you so sure they cannot gain it, if they don't have to waste time learning how to code? If not now, what about soon-ish?

> At this current year and date, the AI does not automate me in anyway

Not now, granted. But what about soon? In other words, shouldn't you be worried as well as excited?

在 HN 中查看
Aperocky 2026/04/16 02:43
如果你什么都不做，你肯定应该担心，因为不使用 LLM 很快就会变得站不住脚。

如果你做了很多，你就会对一些说法和炒作产生怀疑，并了解这会导致什么结果。

我的立场是，如果有人经常使用LLM，他们对LLM的未来的看法可能是对的，也可能是错的。如果他们不这样做，那么他们肯定是不对的，或者只是幸运的。

我个人的判断是，这两者都是硬上限，直到他们发明了一些不是变压器的东西，基本上是从头开始。

Well if you do nothing you should definitely be worried, because not using LLM is rapidly becoming untenable.

If you do a lot, you'll grow skeptical about some of the claims and hype, and have a sense of where this is leading to.

My position is that if someone use LLM a lot, they maybe right or wrong about the future of LLM. If they don't, then they definitely are not right or are only lucky.

My personal judgement is both of these are hard caps until they invented something that's not a transformer, start from scratch bascially.

在 HN 中查看
the_af 2026/04/16 03:31
> 因为不使用LLM很快就变得站不住脚了

完全同意。这不是我所提倡的。当然，人工智能公司有很多自私的炒作（危言耸听可能是另一种炒作）。但 一些 我认为它会是真的，或者足够多的公司会相信它是真的，这都是一样的。

我只是担心，我无能为力。我并不是说“不要使用人工智能”，我只是在反驳那种鲁莽的“兴奋”感觉。

> because not using LLM is rapidly becoming untenable

Completely agreed. This is not what I'm advocating for. And definitely, there's a lot of self-serving hype (and fearmongering can be another kind of hype) by AI companies. But some of it I think will be true, or enough companies will believe it to be true, which amounts to the same.

I'm just worried, I cannot help it. And I'm not saying "don't use AI", I'm pushing back about the feeling of reckless "excitement".

ChrisLTD 2026/04/16 02:42
您认为这些问题很快就会得到解决吗？即使你的老板不需要学习编码，他们是否还有时间在其他任务之上进行人工智能争论工作？
Does it seem to you like those issues will be solved soon? Does your boss have the time to do this AI wrangling work on top of their other tasks even if they don't have to learn to code?
在 HN 中查看
the_af 2026/04/16 03:32
> 您认为这些问题很快就会得到解决吗？

不。

但在 GPT 炒作的早期阶段，我也对 AI 是否能够半可靠地编写代码非常怀疑，看看我现在的处境：我生成的大部分代码都是由 AI 编写的。所以我之前错了，这让我怀疑自己预测不久将来的能力。

> 即使你的老板不需要学习编码，他们是否还有时间在其他任务之上进行人工智能争论工作？

我老板的老板可能很想除掉我 和 我的直接上司。一整类问题都会消失，从而使链条上层的人有时间专注于这个问题……无论是他们还是一小群工程师，这都会让我失业。我已经看到小商店里的人被解雇了，因为他们直接的半技术老板现在可以用人工智能来完成他们的工作（由于隐私原因不能透露细节。而且，最终结果是否有缺陷并不重要，重要的是“任务完成”并且有人失业了）。

> Does it seem to you like those issues will be solved soon?

No.

But I was also very skeptical about AI being able to code semi-reliably during the early stages of GPT hype, and look where I'm now: most of the code I produce is written by an AI. So I was wrong before, which makes me doubt my own ability to predict the near future.

> Does your boss have the time to do this AI wrangling work on top of their other tasks even if they don't have to learn to code?

My boss' boss would probably love to get rid of both me and my direct boss. And a whole class of problems will disappear, freeing time of people higher up the chain to focus on this... either them or a tiny group of engineers, which leaves me out of a job either way. I've already seen people in small shops get fired because their immediate semi-technical boss can now do their job with AI (cannot go details because of privacy reasons. Also, it doesn't matter if the end result is flawed, it matters that "mission accomplished" and someone is out of a job).

在 HN 中查看
ChrisLTD 2026/04/16 06:16
> 但在 GPT 炒作的早期阶段，我也非常怀疑 AI 是否能够半可靠地编码，看看我现在的处境：我生成的大部分代码都是由 AI 编写的

几年前我的印象是，它在编码方面相当不错，只是从问题到代码的速度很慢，而且围绕它的工具已经显着改进，因此一切都非常快。我认为这些模型是否从根本上更擅长原始编码是一个更模糊的问题。

他们仍然会在更大的架构任务中失败、出轨、产生幻觉等等。因此，在我看来，这似乎是当前技术的核心问题。

> 最终结果是否有缺陷并不重要，重要的是“任务完成”并且某人失业了

这是一个短期问题。如果市场还有理智的话，那些拥有执行力的人才的商店将胜过那些目光短浅的商店。

> But I was also very skeptical about AI being able to code semi-reliably during the early stages of GPT hype, and look where I'm now: most of the code I produce is written by an AI

My impression from a couple years ago was that it was fairly decent at coding, it was just slow to go from question -> code, and the tooling around that has improved significantly so that it's all pretty quick. I think whether or not the models are fundamentally better at raw coding is a murkier question.

They still fall down at bigger architectural tasks, go off the rails, hallucinate, etc. So, it seems to me like a core problem with the current technology.

> it doesn't matter if the end result is flawed, it matters that "mission accomplished" and someone is out of a job

This is a short term problem. If the market has any sanity left, the shops that maintain the talent to execute well will out-perform the shops that were short-sighted.

在 HN 中查看
the_af 2026/04/16 06:41
> 几年前我的印象是，它在编码方面相当不错，只是从问题到代码的速度很慢，而且周围的工具已经有了显着的改进，所以一切都很快

你的经历和我的很不一样。早期的 GPT/LLM 技术是错误的。众所周知，它会凭空产生幻觉代码，一直进行重大更改，未能遵循非常简单的指令。我记得它不能玩井字棋的时候！它产生了董事会立场和规则的幻觉。我以前总是为了好玩而把它弄坏（而且没花多少时间，它大多是自己从楼梯上掉下来的）。现在它可以玩更复杂的游戏。

我的怀疑是否正确？嗯，根据我所看到的，我是对的。 GPT 令人印象深刻且有趣，但大多数时候也犯了可笑的错误。直到他们不再是为止！

> My impression from a couple years ago was that it was fairly decent at coding, it was just slow to go from question -> code, and the tooling around that has improved significantly so that it's all pretty quick

Your experience is very different from mine. Early GPT/LLM tech was hilariously wrong. It famously hallucinated code out of nowhere, made breaking changes all the time, failed to follow very simple instructions. I remember when it couldn't play Tic Tac Toe! It hallucinated board positions and rules. I used to break it all the time, for fun (and it didn't take much, it mostly fell down the stairs on its own). Now it can play far more complex games.

Was I right to be skeptical? Well, based on what I saw, I was right. GPT was impressive and fun but also hilariously wrong most of the time. Until they weren't!

在 HN 中查看
ChrisLTD 2026/04/16 06:20
是的，这是一个显而易见的问题。

我们以前也经历过这样的循环。过去，Dreamweaver 会让所有 Web 开发人员失业。最近，Squarespace 也打算做类似的事情。然而，一旦你走出了老路，你就会遇到更难调试的问题，或者你想要一些工具不知道或设计来处理的定制，现在你再次雇用或支付专家费用。

Yes, this is the obvious problem.

We've been through cycles like this before. Back in the day, Dreamweaver was going to put every web developer out of a job. More recently, Squarespace was going to do something similar. However, as soon as you step out of the well-trodden path, you're encountering tougher to debug issue, or you want some customization that the tools aren't aware of or designed to handle, and now you're hiring or paying a specialist again.

在 HN 中查看
the_af 2026/04/16 06:39
> 我们以前也经历过这样的循环。过去，Dreamweaver 会让每个 Web 开发人员失业 [...]

我明白你在说什么。这就是为什么我最初也持怀疑态度。但请考虑一下：这一次， 质量上 不同的是，更重要的是，公司似乎相信这一点，这对我们的工作产生了真正的影响。

Dreamweaver 从未威胁过我的工作。一次也没有。 Squarespace 也没有。我确信他们确实威胁过 一些 工作，但最终他们只是没有取代指导他们的思想和双手，事实上，他们从来没有以此为目标。对于许多实际用例，“无代码”工具同样会被误导。然而，这一次，人工智能似乎在这方面取得了真正的进展，并且正在成为对就业的真正威胁。

“但是当计算器/写作/$SOME_OTHER_TECH 被引入时......”的论点不符合我的观点。 $OLD_TECH 不一定类似于新技术，尤其是人工智能。

如果什么 这次 有什么不同吗？

> We've been through cycles like this before. Back in the day, Dreamweaver was going to put every web developer out of a job [...]

I get what you're saying. This is why I was also skeptical, initially. But consider this: this time, it's qualitatively different, and more importantly, companies seem to believe so, which has real impact on our jobs.

Dreamweaver never threatened my job. Not once. Neither did Squarespace. I'm sure they did threatened some jobs, but ultimately they simply didn't replace the mind and hands guiding them, and in fact, they never aimed at this. "No code" tools were similarly misguided for a lot of real use case. However, this time, AI seems to be making real progress towards this, and is becoming a real threat to jobs.

The argument of "but when calculators/writing/$SOME_OTHER_TECH was introduced..." don't fly with me. $OLD_TECH is not necessarily analogous to new tech, or AI in particular.

What if this time it's different?

在 HN 中查看
ChrisLTD 2026/04/16 08:28
科技公司的营销部门预测了过去两次技术革命中的 10 次。 （加密货币是货币的未来，MOOC 将扼杀大学，Zoom 将扼杀办公室，自动驾驶汽车将终结个人汽车所有权）

此外，企业因人工智能而裁员，而不是整体市场疲软（关税、能源成本上升）和资源配置不当（对虚拟现实、加密货币的过度投资），这有点太方便了。

The marketing departments at tech companies have predicted 10 out of the last 2 technology revolutions. (Crypto is the future of money, MOOCs will kill universities, Zoom will kill the office, self driving cars will end personal automobile ownership)

Also, it’s a little too convenient that businesses are getting to spin their layoffs as a result of AI, rather than a weakening overall market (tariffs, higher energy costs) and a misallocation of resources (over-investment in VR, crypto).

Yeah, no one ever thinks beyond "whoa, how cool, I cloned Slack in 15 minutes!"

Personally, the thing I find more depressing is turning a career that was primarily about solving interesting puzzles in elegant ways into managing a swarm of idiot savant chatbots with "OK, that looks good" or "no, do it better" commands.

quantified 2026/04/15 23:57
一切似乎都有道理，但并不是很有变革性。就像想象一下，汽车带来的新工作包括汽车维修、轮胎店等等。旅行护士、汽车旅馆经营者、军用坦克、门板、郊区生活、NASCAR 的啤酒销售，这些都是由汽车（及其更大的兄弟姐妹卡车）实现的。 仍然缺少的是“人工智能”所带来的就业和行业，而这些就业和行业本身并不是“人工智能”。
All plausible, but not very transformative. Like imagining that the new jobs enabled for the automobile include automobile maintenance, tire shops, and so on. Traveling nurses, motel operators, military tanks, doordash, suburban life, beer sales at NASCAR, those were all enabled by the car (and its larger sibling the truck). Still missing are the jobs snd industries enabled by "AI" that are not themselves "AI".
在 HN 中查看
yubblegum 2026/04/16 09:10
不知何故，今天早上早些时候读完OP后，我走上了一条通往“阿博诺提库斯的亚历山大——2世纪希腊的假先知”的线索[1]。萨莫萨塔 (Samorsata) 的某个卢西安 (Lucian) [2] 写了关于此人的唯一已知传记 [3]，读起来很有趣。

现在，这个家伙对每个预言机收取 1 德拉克马和 6 奥博尔（显然约为 100-150 美元）的费用。这就是他的“象征性利率”。而他的客户“胖子”（卢锡安的措辞，不是我的/g）对这个骗局如此着迷，以至于这个家伙每年赚取高达 80,000 德拉克马！这已经超过1000万美元了。为了吐废话。

至于为什么这一切与OP的文章有很大关系，他提到律师因为使用LLM输出而不是他们的大脑而陷入麻烦......

“为了给人们留下更深刻的印象，他宣布他将诱导上帝说话，并在没有中介的情况下给出他的回应……这些神谕被称为自动电话，不会随意授予任何人，而是保留给官员、富人和奢侈的人。

“这是一部手机，是关于亚美尼亚入侵的事情送给 Severiann [4] 的。他用这些台词鼓励他：

“亚美尼亚，帕提亚，被你凶猛的长矛吓倒了， 前往罗马，前往台伯河的闪亮波浪，你来了， 你的眉毛周围环绕着树叶和光芒四射的金色！

“然后，当愚蠢的高卢人接受了他的建议并入侵时，奥特里阿德斯彻底摧毁了他自己和他的军队，这位顾问从他的档案中删除了这个神谕[！！！]并替换了以下内容：

“不要为亚美尼亚的土地烦恼；它不会繁荣； 一个穿着柔软衣服的人将从他的弓射出死亡， 并切断你与生命和光明的联系。’”

[1]： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_of_Abonoteichus

[2]： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucian_of_Samosata

[3]： https://topostext.org/work/337

[4]： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcus_Sedatius_Severianus

Somehow ended up after reading OP earlier this morning on a trail that lead to "Alexander of Abonoteichus – The False Prophet of 2nd Century Greece" [1]. A certain Lucian of Samorsata [2] wrote up the only known bio on the man [3] and it makes for interesting reading.

Now this guy was charging 1 drachma and 6 obols (apparently ~$100-150) per oracle. So that was his 'token rate'. And his clients the "fat-heads" (Lucian's phrasing not mine /g) were so taken by the scam that this guy earned up to 80,000 drachma per year! That's over 10 million bucks. For spewing b.s.

As to why all this is remotely related to OP's article, his mention of lawyers getting into trouble because of using LLM output instead of their brains ...

"By way of impressing the people still more, he announced that he would induce the God to speak, and give his responses without an intermediary ... These oracles were called autophones, and were not vouchsafed casually to any one, but reserved for officials, the rich, and the lavish.

"It was an autophone which was given to Severiann [4] regarding the invasion of Armenia. He encouraged him with these lines:

'Armenia, Parthia, cowed by thy fierce spear, To Rome, and Tiber's shining waves, thou com'st, Thy brow with leaves and radiant gold encircled!'

"Then when the foolish Gaul took his advice and invaded, to the total destruction of himself and his army by Othryades, the adviser expunged that oracle from his archives [!!!] and substituted the following:

"'Vex not th' Armenian land; it shall not thrive; One in soft raiment clad shall from his bow Launch death, and cut thee off from life and light.'"

[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_of_Abonoteichus

[2]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucian_of_Samosata

[3]: https://topostext.org/work/337

[4]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcus_Sedatius_Severianus

embedding-shape 2026/04/15 23:12
> 在我看来，一个小型博客网站不会因此而被拉起

好吧，也许不是典型的工程博客，但我认为如果你是一个清教徒，Aphyr 的一些帖子/文本可能会达到“成人内容”的边缘，所以我并不感到惊讶，Aphyr 宁愿谨慎行事，同时也表达自己的观点。

> IMO a small blog website is not going to get pulled-up for this

nemomarx 2026/04/15 23:41
这可能是一个政治观点，但我认为你的比较过度推销了对一个小国家进行地理封锁是多么不方便，以及如果发生任何事情会令人头痛。真的不比什么都不做多付出多少努力吗？

显然，英国的用户如果愿意的话可以找到自己的阅读方式，因此成本也很小。

Forgeties79 2026/04/15 22:51
永远不知道您的一篇帖子何时会受到广泛关注。不值得冒这个风险。很容易找到很多例子，人们在做决定时会想着“我/我们永远不会足够大，以至于无法做到这一点”，但后来却被这个决定所困扰。典型例子：朋友和家人之间关于小事的合作协议/合同。
Never know when one of your posts might gain serious traction. Not worth the risk. Very easy to find many examples of people making decisions thinking “I/we will never be big enough for that to be relevant” only to be haunted by that decision later. Classic example: partnership agreements/contracts between friends and family on small endeavors.

在 HN 中查看
keyes343 2026/04/16 00:18
有了人工智能，你只需要在缓慢行动和稍后犯下巨大错误之间做出选择。

如果你走得快，稍后你一定会遇到人工智能错误。然后你最终会放慢速度来修复它们。这需要更多时间。

这个黑匣子将不断发展。人工智能翻译员必须不断跟上它。

With AI, you just have to choose between going slow vs making a huge blunder later at some point.

If you go fast, you are bound to come across AI bugs later. Then you ultimately slow down to fix them. Which takes more time.

That black box will keep evolving. The AI interpreter will have to keep catching up with it.

keeda 2026/04/16 01:35

“专攻昆虫。” （或者对于那些突破人类知识界限的人来说。）

I think, long term, there will be only one job comprising all these aspects. The only absolutely-minimum-required skill will be critical thinking, possibly along with data / statistical literacy for grounding.

"Specialization is for insects." (Or for someone pushing the boundaries of human knowledge.)

abstracthinking 2026/04/15 23:14
人类将承担责任，而不是机器，无论使用什么技术。您建议的工作是基于目前法学硕士的状态，考虑到进展状态，这可能会迅速改变。这些只是使用这些仪器的人员已经完成的活动，因为他们希望优化并从这些机器获得最佳/最安全的输出。
Humans will be held accountable, not machines, whatever is the technology used. The jobs you suggest are based on the state of LLM right now, this could change rapidly, considering the state of progress. These are just activities that are already done by people that work with these instruments, because they want to optimize and obtain the best/safest output from these machines.

anonyfox 2026/04/16 01:43
有责任保险的工作是最后实现自动化的工作。即使它整天不做任何事情或“工作”。但当法律规定出现问题时，经常有人需要进监狱。在某些情况下，“哎呀，是电脑干的”并不是一个充分的借口，责任是一回事。因此，虽然我们也可以机械地用机器取代首席执行官，并且可以说有时甚至比外面的几个人更好，但无论技术有多好，可以拖上法庭的机构都是强制性的。
jobs that have liability coverage are the last ones to get automated. even if its not doing anything all day long in terms or "work". but someone needs to go to jail when shit goes wrong as mandate by law quite often. the "oops the computer did it" isn't a sufficient excuse in some situations, accountability is a thing. so while we mechanically could replace also CEOs with machines and arguably at times would be even better than a few humans out there, some body that can be dragged to court is mandatory no matter how good the tech gets.

elcapitan 2026/04/15 22:02
有没有办法重新称呼人类为人类而不是“肉”？或者反社会的杰弗里·达默现在已经成为新常态？
Is there a way back to calling human beings human beings and not "meat"? Or is the sociopathic Jeffrey Dahmer undertone now the new normal?
在 HN 中查看
crote 2026/04/15 23:04
那是 确切地 为什么使用这个术语。

像亚马逊这样的公司并不将仓库工人视为人。工人们被视为一次性的：被迫在瓶子里撒尿，被迫在倒下的同事的尸体周围工作，支付绝对最低的工资，并取代了 第二 它们的运转不像一台完美无故障的机器。你不被视为人，你是一个工具。牛。他们被迫保留一块肉，因为机器人还不能完全完成你的任务。

这篇文章对“肉盾”的使用没有什么不同。人类 是 被雇用的唯一原因是对人工智能决定的行为负责。他们之所以存在只是因为公司不能将责任归咎于机器，如果根本没有人可以责怪，就会被起诉至遗忘。你作为一个人的存在是无关紧要的，他们只是对那些有心跳的人感兴趣，当事情不可避免地出现问题时，他们可以责怪他们。

That is exactly why the term is being used.

A company like Amazon doesn't treat its warehouse workers as human beings. Workers are seen as disposable: forced to piss in bottles, forced to work around the corpses of their collapsed coworkers, paid the absolute minimum possible, and replaced the second they don't operate like a perfect unfailing machine. You aren't viewed like a human, you are a tool. Cattle. A piece of meat they are forced to retain because a robot isn't quite capable of doing your task yet.

The article's use of "meat shields" isn't any different. Humans are going to be hired for the sole reason of taking accountability for actions dictated by AI. They are there only because the company can't put blame on a machine and will be sued to oblivion if there's nobody to blame at all. Your existence as a person is irrelevant, they are just interested in someone with a heartbeat they can blame when stuff inevitably goes wrong.

在 HN 中查看
chii 2026/04/15 23:42
> 当事情不可避免地出现问题时，他们可以责备那些心跳加速的人。

如果这个人可以受到指责（并承担责任），但不能停止行动或审计行动，采取预防措施（这要花钱）等，那么他们就不能承担真正的责任，因此责任是否落在纸面上是无关紧要的——如果有真正的惩罚（比如入狱），但没有真正的权力来执行任何事情，那么谁会愚蠢到承担这项工作？如果没有真正的惩罚，那纸上的罪名又有什么关系呢？

> someone with a heartbeat they can blame when stuff inevitably goes wrong.

if said person can be blamed (and take on the liability), but cannot stop the action or audit the action, take preventative measures (which costs money) etc, then they cannot take responsibility for real and thus whether the blame falls on them on paper is irrelevant - if there's real punishment (like jail time), but no real power to enforce anything, then who would be stupid enough to take on this job? If there's no real punishment, then what does it matter that the blame on paper is there?

nonameiguess 2026/04/15 22:28
这是由 10 部分组成的系列中的第 9 部分。在过去 9 天里，作者每天都会将每一章发布到 Hacker News 上。每次前五个左右评论中有四个是：

有人指出它在英国不可用。

有人发布了存档。是链接。

有人问为什么上面发布了一个静态站点的存档链接。

答案是因为该内容在英国不可用。

我们真的需要看到这个吗 每一个 时间？

我意识到我现在也没有加入真正的讨论，但是天哪，这太令人恼火了。我们是否可以制定一条新规则，即发布自己内容的作者在知道在英国不可用的情况下，必须发布自己的存档链接并解释为什么他们这样做作为提交的一部分？

This is part 9 of a 10-part series. The author has posted every chapter to Hacker News every day for the past 9 days. Every time four of the first five or so comments are:

Someone noting it is unavailable in the UK.

Someone posting an archive.is link.

Someone asking why the above posted an archive link to a static site.

An answer that it is because the content is otherwise unavailable in the UK.

Do we really need to see this every single time?

I realize I am also not adding to the real discussion now as well, but Jesus Christ, this is irritating. Can we get a new rule that an author posting their own content, knowing it is unavailable in the UK, has to post their own archive link and explain why they're doing so as part of the submission?

pixl97 2026/04/16 00:05
你问错了问题。

英国政府是否有合法权利对任何因英国访问者访问该网站而可能违反其法律的网站进行国际刑事调查？

回答是或否，这是一个简单的二元问题，不需要任何概率思维。

You're asking the wrong question.

Does the UK government have the legal right to do an international criminal investigation against any website that is potentially violating their laws by having visitors from the UK accessing the site?

Answer yes or no, this is an easy binary question, and not one that requires any probabilistic thinking.
